name: TBC169 synbiotic improved chronic constipation outcomes with exploratory microbiome shifts
summary: A new middle-aged and elderly constipation RCT provides disease-specific human symptom evidence plus post-intervention microbiome composition signals, but it does not establish broad aging-functional benefit.
statement: In 140 participants aged at least 45 years with chronic constipation, a Heyndrickxia coagulans TBC169 synbiotic plus dietary-fiber components improved weekly bowel movements, stool-form scores, Wexner scores, and constipation quality-of-life scores versus dietary fiber alone, with post-intervention microbiota profiles showing higher diversity and different community composition.
population_or_model: Middle-aged and elderly adults with chronic constipation
quantitative_note: The PubMed abstract reports significant improvement in weekly bowel movements, Bristol stool scale, Wexner score, and PAC-QOL, plus higher post-intervention Shannon and Faith_PD diversity in the synbiotic group.
caveats: The outcome is chronic constipation symptom burden, not frailty, intrinsic capacity, disability, disease prevention, lifespan, or mortality.
The intervention combines probiotic and fiber components, so the active component cannot be isolated.
Baseline fecal samples were not collected, so microbiota composition findings are exploratory between-group associations.
The source reports mild, transient adverse events but not long-term durability.
